_meta.note: Seed folk tales for storyMatch.ts — grows via folk_tale_collector.py into ml/data/folk_tales/

tales.title: Anansi the Spider
tales.synopsis: Anansi, a clever spider, trades wisdom, shares food, and outwits stronger animals. Every bargain is a count, a share, or a fair split — and the community watches whether the math of fairness holds.
tales.setting: Forest villages of the Gold Coast
tales.katha_voice_sample: Anansi pours the stew into five clay bowls — Tiger watches every ladle, because tonight fairness is survival.

tales.title: The Master Drummer
tales.synopsis: Kwame learns polyrhythms at the drumming academy. Each pattern is a living fraction — three beats against four, halves against wholes — and the master will not let him play the ceremony until the counts lock.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The master taps four on the rim while Kwame must answer with three inside — miss one pulse and the whole court hears it.

tales.title: The Kente Loom
tales.synopsis: Adjoa's family weaving business receives a palace commission. Each Kente strip encodes sequence and symmetry — one broken ratio in the pattern and the elders send the cloth back.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Adjoa counts thirteen gold threads against seven crimson — the palace pattern does not forgive a single line out of place.

tales.title: The Wayfinder
tales.synopsis: Hina navigates an outrigger between islands using stars, swells, and wind. Bearing and distance are not poetry — miscount the angle and the crew runs out of water.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Hina marks the star rise against the swell interval — if the angle drifts two finger-widths, the island they need will slide past in the dark.

tales.title: The Calendar Keepers
tales.synopsis: Maya tends a stone observatory. The Venus alignment ceremony cannot be a day early or late — base-twenty counts and long cycles must close exactly.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The Venus table has held for ninety cycles — one miscounted day and the priests declare the harvest cursed.

tales.title: House of Wisdom
tales.synopsis: Riyad copies manuscripts in Baghdad's great library. Completing the square is literal — al-Khwarizmi's pages settle inheritance disputes with exact areas and roots.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The courtyard must be squared before the estate divides — Riyad's charcoal diagram is the only deed the judge will accept.

tales.title: The Merchant of Pisa
tales.synopsis: Leonardo keeps ledgers on the Pisa waterfront. Arabic numerals and unit ratios decide which cargo clears customs before the tide turns.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The customs officer wants the load in Venetian pounds but the manifest is in Florentine — Leonardo has until the tide to make them agree.

tales.title: The Sulbasutras
tales.synopsis: Apala lays out a sacrifice altar with rope-stretching geometry. The fire pit must be a perfect right triangle — one skewed corner angers the priests.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Apala pulls the cord three times the east side and four times the north — the diagonal must close before sunset ignites the rite.

tales.title: Florence Nightingale's Wards
tales.synopsis: Florence records mortality in Scutari's hospital wards. The numbers on her polar diagrams are lives — pattern in the data decides which ward gets clean water first.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Florence inks another row into the ward ledger — the east wing's death rate is double the west, and the committee meets at dawn.

tales.title: The Clever Jackal
tales.synopsis: A jackal divides a kill among hungry animals. Each claims a share by rank and appetite — only careful fractions keep the peace under the banyan tree.
tales.setting: Forest court under a banyan tree
tales.katha_voice_sample: The jackal marks the carcass into six equal strips — the lion will take two, but only if the hyenas agree on what one-sixth looks like.

tales.title: The Merchant and the Genie
tales.synopsis: A merchant dates his debts and travel days across desert routes. One wrong sum on the ledger and the caravan leaves without him.
tales.setting: Desert caravan route, medieval Islamic world
tales.katha_voice_sample: The caravan master counts forty nights of water against sixty leagues of sand — the merchant's debt must clear before the bells at dawn.

tales.title: Coyote and the Stars
tales.synopsis: Coyote scatters stars across the sky. Elders say the pattern must hold so hunters can navigate — each constellation is a counted map of seasons.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Coyote lifts the last handful of stars — if the winter triangle is one point short, the hunters will walk into the wrong canyon.

tales.title: The Boatman's Tide
tales.synopsis: A boatman times the tide through mangrove channels. Miss the slack water by twenty minutes and the tiger marsh closes the only route home.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The tide chart says slack in forty-one minutes — the boatman's pole will not reach bottom if he enters the narrow channel too early.

tales.title: The Thousand Cranes
tales.synopsis: Sadako folds cranes wishing for healing. Each fold is symmetry and count — the legend says a thousand, and the hospital wall tracks every one.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Crane six hundred and twelve sits beside six hundred and eleven — Sadako will not stop until the wall shows a thousand exact folds.

tales.title: The Igloo Builder
tales.synopsis: A hunter spirals snow blocks into a dome. Each block's angle and overlap must close the curve before the blizzard — geometry is shelter.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The last block must lean inward twelve finger-widths — if the spiral opens, the wind will take the lamp in one breath.

tales.title: Fionn and the Causeway
tales.synopsis: Fionn builds a stone path across the sea to face a rival giant. Each basalt column must match height and spacing or the path sinks before he crosses.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Fionn sets the sixth column one span taller than the fifth — the Scottish giant is already counting footsteps on the far shore.

tales.title: Māori Star Compass
tales.synopsis: A navigator reads a star compass carved in memory. Each rising point marks a latitude band — confuse two stars and the waka misses Aotearoa.
tales.katha_voice_sample: When Antares clears the swell by two hand-widths, the navigator knows the latitude line — miss it and the homeland stars never rise.

tales.title: The Coffee Ceremony
tales.synopsis: During the jebena ceremony, hosts measure water, beans, and time in three rounds. Hospitality is arithmetic — each cup must be equal and the last round sweetest.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The third pour must split evenly among seven guests — the host watches the jebena lip, because shame lives in an uneven cup.

tales.title: The Frost Giant's Riddle
tales.synopsis: Thor must answer a giant's riddles at Utgard. One asks how far he walked — the answer is half the remaining path each step, a halving that never quite ends.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Each step Thor takes is half the distance left — the giant smiles because infinity fits inside a riddle.

tales.title: Lo Shu Magic Square
tales.synopsis: A turtle emerges from the Lo River bearing a pattern of dots. Scholars arrange the numbers so every row and diagonal sums the same — balance is cosmic law.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Every row on the turtle's shell must close to fifteen — Yu copies the dots before the flood returns.

tales.title: The Capoeira Circle
tales.synopsis: In the roda, two capoeiristas move to the berimbau's rhythm. Angles of entry and timing are geometry in motion — one misread beat and you're on the sand.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The berimbau speeds one beat — the newcomer's sweep is three degrees too wide and the circle gasps as one.

tales.title: Brigid's Counting Cattle
tales.synopsis: Brigid tends cattle through Imbolc. Milk yields are logged by herd and day — the abbess needs exact totals before the spring market.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Twelve cows, four days, seven pails — Brigid's tally decides which orphanage gets milk before the frost lifts.

tales.title: The Three Princes of Serendip
tales.synopsis: Three princes infer hidden facts from clues on the road. Each deduction chains observations — probability and logic, not luck, solve the lost camel.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The princes note blind teeth, no milk, and a single track — the lost camel was neither old nor nursing, and walked alone.

tales.title: The Market Vendor
tales.synopsis: A vendor in Port-au-Prince prices mangoes by weight and bundle. The storm knocked out power — only mental math keeps the stall honest before the rain returns.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The scale is dead but the customer wants three kilos at forty gourdes — the vendor counts mangoes by hand before the sky breaks.

tales.title: The Turtle and the Hare
tales.synopsis: A turtle bets a boastful hare on a river crossing. Steady rate beats burst speed — the turtle's constant pace graphs a line the hare cannot outrun.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The turtle crosses at one steady arm-stroke per breath — the hare's sprint graph peaks early and fades before the far bank.

tales.title: Dreamtime Star Songlines
tales.synopsis: Elders sing songlines that map country to stars. Each verse is a counted step on the land — lose the sequence and the waterhole story vanishes.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The fourth verse turns east at the emu constellation — the young ones must count the beats or the waterhole stays hidden.

tales.title: The Nested Dolls
tales.synopsis: A carver paints matryoshka dolls that fit inside each other. Each shell is a fixed ratio smaller — one wrong circumference and the inner doll rattles loose.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The fifth doll must be exactly seventy percent the height of the fourth — the carver's calipers click until the fit is silent.

tales.title: The Pulque Ferment
tales.synopsis: A farmer tracks agave sap fermentation by day and temperature. The ritual calendar demands the brew peak on the feast — exponential growth, not guesswork.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The sap doubled sweetness in three days last year — tonight's feast needs the curve to peak at sunset, not noon.

tales.title: Nasreddin Hodja's Donkey
tales.synopsis: Hodja rides his donkey backward to teach a lesson. The village argues about distance traveled versus direction faced — vectors before anyone named them.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Hodja travels east on a donkey facing west — the village counts miles forward while arguing which way wisdom went.

tales.title: The Rice Terraces
tales.synopsis: Farmers carve Banaue terraces into the mountain. Each level must hold water at the same depth — slope, area, and flow decide whether the village eats.
tales.katha_voice_sample: Water must sit level across twelve steps of the mountain — one terrace off by a finger's width and the lowest field drinks nothing.

tales.title: Odysseus and the Stars
tales.synopsis: Lost at sea, Odysseus steers by Pleiades and Bear. Night after night the angles change predictably — miss the pattern and Ithaca stays invisible.
tales.katha_voice_sample: When the Bear clears the mast by a palm's width, Odysseus knows the night row — drift one degree and the island smoke never shows.

tales.title: The Tournament Draft
tales.synopsis: Remy's team drafts for a LAN final. Expected value on picks and counter-picks is math — emotion drafts losers.
tales.katha_voice_sample: The opponent first-picks the meta tank — Remy's sheet says seventy-one percent win rate if they ban the healer next.

tales.title: The Five-Story Temple and Its Ten Guardians
tales.synopsis: The five-story pagoda of Nyatapola in Bhaktapur rises above the city on a stepped plinth guarded by ten stone figures set in pairs on the stairway, and each pair below is said to be ten times as strong as the pair above it. Two famous wrestlers guard the bottom step, then elephants ten times their strength, then lions stronger still, then griffins, and finally the goddess Baghini at the very top, so that whoever wants to reach the temple's summit has to pass a stairway that gets more powerful the higher it goes even as the guardians themselves get smaller.

characters.name: the paired stone guardians
characters.role: wrestlers, elephants, lions, griffins, and a goddess

tales.setting: The stepped stone plinth of Nyatapola temple in Bhaktapur's Taumadhi square
tales.katha_voice_sample: Two wrestlers guard the bottom step. Elephants ten times their strength stand above them, then lions, then griffins, each pair smaller and mightier than the last.

tales.title: Arniko Carries the Pagoda East
tales.synopsis: In the thirteenth century, a young Newar architect named Arniko led a team of craftsmen from the Kathmandu Valley to Tibet and then on to the Yuan court in China, where Kublai Khan asked him to repair a damaged stupa. Arniko instead proposed and built an entirely new one, the great White Stupa of Beijing, translating the valley's tiered pagoda proportions, each story a careful fraction narrower than the one below it, into a structure that still stands in China eight centuries later.

characters.name: Kublai Khan
characters.role: Yuan emperor who commissions the stupa

tales.setting: The Yuan imperial court in thirteenth-century Beijing, after a long journey from the Kathmandu Valley
tales.katha_voice_sample: Arniko carries the valley's pagoda proportions all the way to Kublai Khan's court. Each story of the new stupa comes in a fraction narrower than the one below it.

tales.title: The Salt Caravans of the Kali Gandaki Gorge
tales.synopsis: For centuries, Thakali traders drove long yak caravans up the Kali Gandaki gorge, the deepest in the world, carrying grain and rice north into Mustang and Tibet and bringing back blocks of salt in exchange. Every trader kept a careful private ratio in his head, how many measures of grain a single block of salt was worth that season, because the exchange rate shifted with the harvest and a caravan leader who miscounted a single trade could lose the season's entire profit on the walk home.

characters.name: the Thakali caravan leaders
characters.role: traders who set the grain-for-salt exchange rate

tales.setting: The Kali Gandaki gorge, the ancient salt route between Mustang and the Tibetan plateau
tales.katha_voice_sample: So many measures of grain for one block of salt, and the rate shifts with every harvest. Miscount it once and the whole season's walk was for nothing.

tales.title: The Terraces That Climb the Mountain
tales.synopsis: In the middle hills of Nepal, where almost no ground is naturally flat, farming families cut their steep slopes into long stepped terraces, each one a narrow shelf of level soil held in place by a stone wall built to exactly the height the slope demands. A family passes down not just the land but the precise layout of the walls from parent to child, because a terrace built one hand's width too shallow floods in the monsoon, while one built too deep wastes ground that could have grown another row of rice.

characters.name: the hill farming families
characters.role: build and maintain the stepped terraces

tales.setting: The steep middle hills of Nepal during the monsoon planting season
tales.katha_voice_sample: Each terrace wall is built to exactly the height the slope demands. One hand's width too shallow and the monsoon floods the whole shelf of soil.

tales.title: The House Built From a Single Tree
tales.synopsis: In the twelfth century, builders in Kathmandu's central square set out to raise a great pavilion for travelers and needed enough timber for its three tiered roofs. According to the story they felled one single enormous sal tree and shaped every beam, pillar, and rafter of the whole hall from it without using a second trunk or a single nail, and the city itself is said to take its name, Kathmandu, from this Kasthamandap, the House of Wood.
tales.setting: Kathmandu's Durbar Square, at the crossing of two old trade roads
tales.katha_voice_sample: One sal tree, three tiered roofs, not a single nail. The carpenters have exactly one trunk's worth of timber to make every beam agree.

tales.title: The Tharu New Year and the Fish of the Rapti
tales.synopsis: In the Terai lowlands along Nepal's southern border, the Tharu people mark their new year, Maghi, by gathering whole villages to fish the shrinking winter channels of rivers like the Rapti using wide communal nets pulled by dozens of hands at once. Whatever is caught in a single sweep of the net is divided among every household that helped pull it, counted out share by share on the riverbank before anyone carries a single fish home.
tales.setting: The shrinking winter channels of the Rapti river during the Maghi festival
tales.katha_voice_sample: The whole net comes up at once, and every household that pulled it gets a share. Nobody carries a single fish home before the count is settled on the bank.

tales.title: The Chariot of Rato Machindranath
tales.synopsis: Each spring the city of Patan builds an enormous wooden chariot from scratch to carry the rain god Rato Machindranath through the streets, stacking a tower of poles and wheels so tall it can catch on temple eaves if the builders misjudge its height even slightly. Hundreds of people haul the chariot by long ropes through narrow lanes over several weeks, and if it tips or stalls partway, elders read the delay as a sign that the coming rains themselves are still being decided.

characters.name: Rato Machindranath
characters.role: rain god carried through the city

characters.name: the chariot builders
characters.role: carpenters who rebuild the tower every year

tales.setting: The old city lanes of Patan during the chariot's weeks-long procession
tales.katha_voice_sample: The tower of poles goes up pole by pole toward the temple eaves. Guess its final height wrong by a hand's width and the whole chariot jams in the lane.

tales.title: Sita and the Bow of Janakpur
tales.synopsis: In Janakpur, in the Nepali Terai, King Janaka raised his daughter Sita and set a single condition for any suitor who wished to marry her, that he must string and bend an enormous bow that no ordinary man could even lift. When the young prince Rama arrived and drew the bow so forcefully that it snapped in two, the marriage was settled on the spot, and Maithil women in Janakpur still paint the walls of their homes with the same precise geometric symmetry, mirrored borders and repeating patterns, that the old wedding songs describe from that day.
tales.setting: The royal court of Janakpur in the Nepali Terai
tales.katha_voice_sample: No suitor can even lift the bow, until Rama draws it back and it snaps in two. Generations later, Mithila women still mirror that same pattern on every wall.

tales.title: The Hundred and Eight Springs of Muktinath
tales.synopsis: At the pilgrimage site of Muktinath high in the Mustang region, cold mountain spring water is channeled through exactly one hundred and eight bull-shaped brass spouts arranged in a curved wall, and pilgrims are expected to pass beneath every single one before their visit is considered complete. Counting the spouts correctly matters as much as the cold itself, since stopping short of a hundred and eight, even by one, is said to leave the pilgrimage unfinished.

characters.name: the pilgrims
characters.role: pass beneath all one hundred and eight spouts

tales.setting: The stone wall of a hundred and eight water spouts at Muktinath
tales.katha_voice_sample: One hundred and eight spouts, and the pilgrim has to pass beneath every single one. Stop at a hundred and seven and the whole walk does not count.

tales.title: The Walled City of Lo Manthang
tales.synopsis: High in the rain-shadow desert of Upper Mustang sits Lo Manthang, a walled city built centuries ago in a near-perfect rectangle, its four corners deliberately squared off so a single watchman standing at the center could see clean sightlines down every street to each gate. The kingdom stayed so remote behind its own walls and mountain passes that outside travelers were barred from entering at all until the late twentieth century, long after the rest of Nepal had opened to the world.

characters.name: the kings of Lo
characters.role: rule from within the walled city

tales.setting: The walled city of Lo Manthang in the high desert of Upper Mustang
tales.katha_voice_sample: The four walls square off into a near perfect rectangle. A single watchman at the center can see clean down every street to every gate at once.

tales.title: Tenzing Norgay's Long Climb to the Summit
tales.synopsis: Tenzing Norgay grew up in the Khumbu region beneath Everest and spent years learning the mountain's moods before the 1953 expedition, when he and Edmund Hillary pushed past camp after camp, each one set a measured number of feet higher and a day's climb apart, toward the summit. On the morning of May 29, after weeks of planning exactly how much oxygen, rope, and food each stage of the ascent would need, the two men became the first climbers confirmed to stand on the true summit of the world's highest peak.
tales.setting: The camps and ridgelines of Everest's southern route through the Khumbu region
tales.katha_voice_sample: Each camp sits a measured number of feet above the last, a day's climb apart. Tenzing has counted the oxygen for every stage before the sun even rises.

tales.title: Ubhauli and Udhauli: The Two Journeys of the Year
tales.synopsis: The Limbu and Rai communities of eastern Nepal mark two great turning points each year with the Sakela festival, Ubhauli in spring when families and even the earth's nourishing spirits are believed to move upward toward the cooler high hills, and Udhauli in autumn when the same movement reverses back down to the warmer lowlands. Dancers trace the pattern with their own footwork, spiraling outward from the center for Ubhauli and back inward for Udhauli, so the dance itself repeats the same yearly cycle the farming calendar depends on.

characters.name: the Sakela dancers
characters.role: trace the seasonal cycle in their footwork

tales.setting: Village dance grounds in the eastern hills of Nepal, at each turn of the farming year
tales.katha_voice_sample: Spring sends everyone spiraling up toward the high hills, and autumn spirals them back down again. The dancers' feet trace the same turn every single year.

tales.title: Prithvi Narayan Shah and the Many Small Kingdoms
tales.synopsis: Before Nepal was one country, the hills west of the Kathmandu Valley were divided into two loose leagues of small kingdoms, remembered as the Baise, the twenty-two, and the Chaubise, the twenty-four, each ruled by its own small raja. Starting from the little hill kingdom of Gorkha, King Prithvi Narayan Shah spent decades absorbing these kingdoms one by one, along with the Kathmandu Valley itself, into a single unified territory, and the counts in the old kingdoms' names are the exact tally historians still use to describe how fragmented the hills were before him.

characters.name: Prithvi Narayan Shah
characters.role: king of Gorkha who unifies the hill kingdoms

characters.name: the Baise and Chaubise rajas
characters.role: the twenty-two and twenty-four smaller kings

tales.setting: The hill kingdoms west of the Kathmandu Valley in the eighteenth century
tales.katha_voice_sample: Twenty two kingdoms in one league, twenty four in the other. Prithvi Narayan Shah is counting down from Gorkha, one small crown at a time.

tales.title: The Mani Wall and the Turning Wheels
tales.synopsis: Along the high trails of Tamang villages in the Nepal Himalaya, long mani walls are built from stones carved with the same sacred mantra, and travelers keep to the left as they pass so as not to walk against the accumulated blessing. Elders count each turn of a handheld prayer wheel and each bead of a mala necklace as one full repetition of the mantra, and a full circuit of prayers is only considered complete once a practitioner has counted all one hundred and eight beads without losing track.

characters.name: the Tamang elders
characters.role: count mantra repetitions on the mala

tales.setting: A mani wall along a high mountain trail in Tamang country
tales.katha_voice_sample: Each bead on the mala is one full repetition of the mantra, and the elder is counting under his breath. Losing track before a hundred and eight means starting over.

tales.title: Manjushri Drains the Lake of Kathmandu
tales.synopsis: Long ago the Kathmandu Valley was a vast lake, and at its center floated a lotus of pure light where a self-arisen flame burned. The bodhisattva Manjushri traveled from China to see it, and cut a single gorge through the valley's southern hills with his sword so the water could drain away, leaving behind fertile land where the lotus hill became the stupa of Swayambhunath.
tales.setting: The primordial lake that once filled the Kathmandu Valley
tales.katha_voice_sample: Manjushri raises his sword over the water. One cut through the southern hills, and the whole lake has to decide where its land begins.

tales.title: The Footprints in the Snow
tales.synopsis: High-altitude herders in the Himalaya have long told of the yeti, a shaggy creature that leaves a trail of oversized footprints in fresh snow near the highest passes, and travelers who find a print will often lay their own boot inside it to compare the size before deciding whether to press on or turn back. Some of the most famous reported prints stretched nearly twice the length of an ordinary boot, and expedition parties still debate what animal, or combination of animals walking in a single trail, could have left a track that size.

characters.name: the yeti
characters.role: creature known only by its tracks

characters.name: the herders and climbers
characters.role: measure the footprints against their own

tales.setting: A high mountain pass in the Himalaya after a fresh snowfall
tales.katha_voice_sample: The climber sets his own boot down inside the print in the snow. It is nearly twice the length, and that is when he decides to turn back.

tales.title: Shankhadhar's Gift of a New Era
tales.synopsis: According to Newar tradition, a merchant named Shankhadhar Sakhwa discovered a field of buried treasure and used the entire fortune to pay off every debt owed by the ordinary people of the Kathmandu Valley in a single day, leaving no household still counting what it owed another. To mark a valley finally starting clean, the year of that debt-clearing became year one of the Nepal Sambat calendar, a count the Newar community still keeps running alongside every other calendar in use today.

characters.name: Shankhadhar Sakhwa
characters.role: merchant who clears the valley's debts

tales.setting: The Kathmandu Valley at the founding of the Nepal Sambat era
tales.katha_voice_sample: Shankhadhar spends his whole buried fortune clearing every debt in the valley in a single day. The next morning, the calendar starts over at year one.

tales.title: The Hidden Valley Sealed by Guru Rinpoche
tales.synopsis: In the remote Dolpo region near Shey Phoksundo, the turquoise lake is said to guard the entrance to a beyul, a hidden valley sealed centuries ago by the teacher Guru Rinpoche, who left behind a fixed number of hidden treasures, termas, to be found only when the world outside needed their teachings most. Villagers describe the valley's entrance as opening only to a traveler who arrives having correctly followed every one of a set sequence of directional signs, since a single wrong turn among the ridgelines leads back out rather than in.

characters.name: Guru Rinpoche
characters.role: seals the hidden valley and its treasures

characters.name: the Dolpo villagers
characters.role: keep the sequence of directions to find it

tales.setting: The turquoise waters of Shey Phoksundo and the hidden valley said to lie beyond it
tales.katha_voice_sample: Guru Rinpoche left a fixed sequence of signs among the ridgelines. Follow them out of order even once and the hidden valley never opens.

tales.title: The Cow Festival for the Grieving
tales.synopsis: After King Pratap Malla's young son died, the grieving queen was told that every family in Kathmandu who had lost someone that year would walk a cow, or a child dressed as one, through the city on a single set day, so she could see she was not alone in her sorrow. The king, hoping to lighten the procession's grief, encouraged comedians and satirists to march alongside the cows making the crowd laugh, and Kathmandu still holds Gai Jatra every year, counting a hard year by the line of cows that walks through it.

characters.name: the grieving queen
characters.role: asks to see other mourning families

tales.setting: The streets of Kathmandu during the Gai Jatra procession
tales.katha_voice_sample: Every family that lost someone this year walks a cow through the street on the same day. The queen counts the line and finally understands she is not alone.

tales.title: The Living Goddess of Kathmandu
tales.synopsis: When a Kumari, a living child goddess, is needed for the temple in Kathmandu, priests search the city's Newar Shakya families for a girl who shows exactly thirty-two specific signs of perfection, from the shape of her eyelashes to the sound of her voice. She must also spend a night alone in a dark room surrounded by ritually presented buffalo heads without showing fear, and only the girl who passes every one of the thirty-two tests takes her seat on the temple's lion throne.
tales.setting: The Kumari Ghar temple-house on Kathmandu's Durbar Square
tales.katha_voice_sample: Thirty two signs, and the priests are checking every one. Miss even the shape of an eyelash and the throne waits for another girl.

tales.title: Garuda's Watch at Changu Narayan
tales.synopsis: On a hilltop above the Kathmandu Valley stands Changu Narayan, said to be the valley's oldest temple, where the god Vishnu is honored alongside his great bird-mount Garuda kneeling in stone before the shrine. Farmers from the villages below still time certain planting days to the temple's festival calendar, treating Garuda's watchful posture as a yearly reminder that the harvest depends on keeping careful accounts, not just good luck.

characters.name: Vishnu
characters.role: god honored at the hilltop shrine

tales.setting: A hilltop temple above the Kathmandu Valley, among terraced fields
tales.katha_voice_sample: Garuda kneels in stone before the shrine, and the farmers below check the temple's calendar before they touch a single seed to the ground.

tales.title: King Yalambar's Silent Vow
tales.synopsis: As the story is told among the Kirat people of Nepal, King Yalambar traveled to witness the great battle of Kurukshetra from the Mahabharata and privately vowed to fight for whichever side turned out to be weaker, so the outcome would be decided by courage rather than numbers. Krishna, wanting to keep the battle's balance intact, tested the king by asking him to sacrifice his own head, and Yalambar agreed without hesitation, watching the entire eighteen-day war unfold through his own severed head propped up on a hill overlooking the field.

characters.name: King Yalambar
characters.role: Kirat king who vows to aid the weaker side

tales.setting: A hilltop overlooking the battlefield of Kurukshetra
tales.katha_voice_sample: King Yalambar has already decided he will fight for whichever side turns out weaker. Krishna wants to know if he means it before the first arrow flies.

tales.title: Indra Jatra: The God Who Fell to Earth
tales.synopsis: The rain god Indra once came down to earth disguised as an ordinary farmer to steal parijat flowers from a Kathmandu garden for his mother, and was caught and bound by the valley's people before they realized who he was. His mother descended searching for him, and in exchange for his release she promised to send morning dew each winter to nourish the crops, so the city now holds the Indra Jatra festival every year, displaying his image publicly so he is never mistaken for a common thief again.

characters.name: Indra
characters.role: rain god disguised as a flower thief

characters.name: Indra's mother
characters.role: searches the valley for her missing son

tales.setting: A flower garden in the Kathmandu Valley, and the streets during the Indra Jatra procession
tales.katha_voice_sample: The farmer they've tied up in the garden turns out to be Indra himself, and now his mother is combing the whole valley looking for him.

tales.title: Anansi and the Wisdom Pot
tales.synopsis: Anansi the spider tries to hoard all the world's wisdom in a single clay pot, climbing a tree to hide it out of everyone's reach. When the pot slips and shatters at the base of the tree, wisdom scatters across the world in pieces — which is why, the story explains, no single person holds all of it and everyone must share what they know.
tales.setting: A West African forest village near a great tree
tales.katha_voice_sample: In a village near

tales.title: Anansi and the Moss-Covered Rock
tales.synopsis: Anansi discovers a strange rock in the forest that knocks out anyone who insults its odd shape, and uses the trick to steal food from every animal who stops to rest near it. Bush Deer figures out the pattern and turns the trap back on Anansi, teaching the greedy spider a lesson about respecting what you don't understand.
tales.setting: A forest path in the Ashanti homeland
tales.katha_voice_sample: Deep in the forest
